Dr. McComas pointed out, that some state and local leaders in egricultural

program so essential and worthwhile to the nation that it will endure the
challenge of the day. Our nation vas born on the premises of free enterprise,
individual freedom and a strong agricultural economy. No world power has long
maintained ite prestige unless it could provide the bulk of its food end fiber.
Even with the misrepresentation of facts so prevalent in the charges end
accusations being hurled at vocational education in general end at agricultural
education specifically, there must be some justification to these affrontse. A
lack of understanding? Incomplete factual information? A desire to make head~
lines or sell nevspapers? Yes, perhaps on occasion each of these have played
a part in stimlating the concept of inadequacy being reflected by our program.
Hovever, ours is e country where freedom of speech and freedom of the press
are considered a part of the American heritage. We would not in any way wish
to suppress these freedoms. At times ve hide behind the alibi that our program
has been and is being hampered by outmoded legislation. If this is an argument
based on fact, why is it so? Whose fault is it? What have we as agricultural
educators, from teacher trainers down to the beginning teacher, done to correct
and keep legislation up-to-date? How often have we complained about the cost

of dues to our professional orgenizations? And then, after paying cur dues,
how often have we decided that someone else can give their time and their
effort to seeing that our interests are represented through these organizations?
Or worse yet, have we sometimes failed to even pay our dues to professionel
organizations from vhich ve expect representation?
As a neophyte with a decade's less experience than Dr. McComas, I would
assert that the responsibility for this short sightedness of the past and its
projection into the present must be shouldered by state leaders in agriculture.
Whet have we as teacher educators done to stimlate interest in these organizations? In the past we have been so occupied with “lecturing” the advantages
of the problem-solving techniques, of pointing out the need to keep our local
programs abreast of the changes in the farming community, and of adhering
rigidly to the Smith-Hughes legislation, that we have lost sight of the
problems developing around us. Others have apparently not been so obsessed.
As 9 result, ve as agricultural educators are gradually awakening to find
ourselves in the position that our nation faced following "Sputnik." We have

much catching up to do. The people who feel that our nation can not maintain
the American heritege and again rise to the fore in technical knowledge and

competence in the field of space are in the minority. Why then, should ve
as teacher educators not demonstrate our belief that we, too, can catch up?
Agriculture, from the day of the early pioneer, has always been a strong
pert of the American heritage. The independent attitude and way of life are
prominent in our thinking. There is strong evidence that it shall continue
to be a necessity for generations to come, unless we shed such habits as
eating, wearing clothes, and seeking shelter from the elements. As long es
this type of need exists, people mist be trained to do the work. Many of us
have therefore been demonstrating not a lack of belief in the need of a
strong program of agricultural education, but e lack of faith in agriculture
itself. .
Tredition is hard to break. At one time there was 6 clear cut need to
have a department of agriculture in every rural commnity to prepare “present
end prospective farmers for proficiency in farming." Today this need still
existe in many rural commnities. However, other needs have developed. :
Nearly a hundred investigstions of the merits of vocational agriculture training as preparation for college level study in ogriculture attest to its value
in this area. Few such trained men will go back to the farm. The unsatisfied
demand for trained workers in the fields of service, production and processing
attest to the need for training at the high school level. Another indication
of the need for training in agriculture is borne out by the increasing :
requests for assistance with londscaring and horticultural problems. This
list could be expanded, but 11 we establish programs which will meet these
demands alone, ve mist have many modifications and changes. Can we meet the
needs or will we fall back into the depths of ‘The Saber Toothed Curriculum?”
R. E. Naugher, Assistant Director of Agricultural Education in Washington
recently stated, “We mist take a new look at where we are going and what tools
we need to get the job done.” Dr. Naugher made this statement while discussing the implications of the "Perkin's Bill." Assuming thet at last federal
legislation is being established not only to provide additional funds for
vocational training but to also broaden the scope of agricultural education,
what guarantee do ve have that agricultural education will receive a fair
share of these funds? What are we as agricultural educators doing to help
state administrators of the new and existing funds recognize the need to continue the support of agricultural education? Now is the time for all good
teacher educators to come to the ald of their program. New programs mist be
outlined, planned, tried and adjusted to meet the changing demand for
agriculture thet is not merely farming, but an agriculture that is a part of
the American heritage. .
